title: Mudroom Bench with Drawers
summary: A six-foot entry bench with three real drawers on full-extension slides — 2x4 frames wrapped in plywood, with a face frame that makes it read as cabinetry.
description:
This bench is a gateway build: it looks like cabinetry, but underneath it's two 2x4 ladder frames stood on edge, skinned in plywood, with drawer boxes riding full-extension slides between them. If you've been screwing 2x4 furniture together and want to graduate toward cabinets, this is the project that teaches drawers without asking you to build a carcass.

The plan's own emphasis is the right one: the two frames must match EXACTLY, because the drawer slides bridge between them — a quarter inch of mismatch and the drawers rack and bind forever. Build the second frame on top of the first. Equally load-bearing advice: the drawer cut list says CUT TO FIT in capital letters. Read your specific slides' instructions, measure your actual openings, and build the boxes to those numbers; the listed dimensions assume standard 1/2"-clearance side-mount slides and true 3/4" stock.

The finishing move — 1x2/1x4 face frame on the cabinet, 1x3 frames on the drawer fronts with an even 1/8" reveal — is what turns shop-grade materials into a piece that lives in the front hall. Take time on those reveals; they're the whole first impression.

tools.note: Ripping the plywood sheet into the two 23-7/8" strips — a straightedge guide is near-mandatory.

Face frames, drawer bottoms, and the top.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4" cabinet grade, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped into two 96" x 23-7/8" strips — sides and top.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: The two frames and the slide spacers.

materials.name: Full-extension side-mount drawer slides
materials.note: Buy FIRST and read their instructions — the drawer boxes are built to the slides, not to the plan.

cutList.note: CUT TO FIT your slides and openings — listed sizes assume 1/2"-clearance slides.
Cut to fit — match your slide length.
Approximate — sized to an even 1/8" reveal.

cutList.part: Drawer face frame stiles, end drawers
Drawer face frame stiles, middle drawer

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood sheet into two 96" x 23 7/8" strips with a straightedge guide clamped to the sheet — freehanding a 96" rip is how the top ends up out of parallel. From one strip cut the top at 72" long; from the other cut two side panels 23 7/8" x 17 1/4". From the five 8 ft 2x4s cut four frame rails at 70 1/2" and eight uprights at 14 1/4" — one rail plus one upright fits on each of four boards and the last four uprights fit the fifth, which leaves roughly 38" of that board for the drawer slide spacers later. From the 1x2s cut the face frame top rail at 72" and four stiles at 13"; the face frame bottom rail is the full 72" length of the 6 ft 1x4, so use it at its factory length with nothing trimmed off. Cut the 1x10 drawer parts and the 1/4" drawer bottoms LAST, after the carcass is built and the slides are in hand — every drawer row in the cut list is marked cut to fit, and the listed sizes use all three 1x10s with almost no slack, so buy a fourth board if your slides call for different numbers. Label everything as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build two IDENTICAL 2x4 frames
steps.body: Each frame is a ladder: two 70 1/2" rails laid flat with four 14 1/4" uprights standing between them, which makes a frame 17 1/4" tall — the same height as the plywood side panels, and 3/4" of top later brings the bench to 18". Fasten with 3" self-tapping screws, or 1 1/2"-setting pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, plus glue at every joint. Build the second frame stacked directly on top of the first, corner to corner and upright over upright, and clamp them together while you fasten: the drawer slides bridge from the front frame to the back frame, so 'identical' here is not a virtue, it is the difference between drawers that glide and drawers that bind forever. Measure both diagonals on each frame and against each other before unstacking them.

steps.title: Skin the ends
steps.body: Stand the two frames parallel and cap each end with a 23 7/8" x 17 1/4" plywood side panel, which sets the bench depth and locks the two frames at a fixed spacing. The panel is 17 1/4" tall, exactly the frame height, and 70 1/2" of rail plus a 3/4" panel at each end comes to 72" — the length of the top, which is your check that the carcass came out right. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, or brads and glue, into the ends of both rails and the end uprights. Check that the assembly is square and that both frames are still parallel before the glue sets, because from here on every drawer opening is measured out of this box.

steps.title: Build the drawer boxes — to your slides, not the list
steps.body: Read your slides' instructions first and measure the three actual bays in the carcass, because the plan's own numbers do not agree with each other here: the 70 1/2" rails with four 3 1/2" uprights give bays of about 18 13/16", the listed drawer parts make a box 20 1/2" wide, and the face frame implies 22" openings. Your measured bay is the only number that counts. For standard 1/2"-clearance side-mount slides, make each box the bay width minus 1", and make the sides as long as the slides. Build each box with the two sides running the full depth and the front and back fitting BETWEEN them, so the box's outside width is the front/back length plus 3/4" at each side, and the bottom is that same outside width by the side length. If you pocket-hole the boxes, put the pocket holes on the INSIDE faces — the outside faces of the box show through the opening every time the drawer is pulled, and nothing later covers them. Bottoms go on with glue and 3/4" brads or narrow-crown staples, and a bottom nailed on square is what keeps the box square for good.

steps.title: Mount the cabinet slide members
steps.body: Cut 2x4 scraps to the height you want each slide to sit and stand them inside the bench as spacers, then rest each cabinet-side slide member on its spacer and screw it to the frame uprights. Using a cut spacer instead of a pencil mark is what guarantees the left and right members of a pair land at exactly the same height, and matched heights are the entire game with drawers. Do all six members off the same pair of spacers, and check each pair with a level along its length before driving the second screw. Work front frame and back frame together for each slide, since the slide has to bridge from one to the other.

steps.title: Mount the drawer members and hang the boxes
steps.body: Attach the drawer-side slide members to the boxes exactly where the slide instructions specify, measuring their setback from the front edge of the box the same on both sides of every drawer. Then lift each box into its cabinet members until the slides click home. Fit the boxes one at a time and try each one before hanging the next, so a problem is traceable to one drawer rather than to three.

steps.title: Tune the fit
steps.body: Cycle every drawer fully. Binding or rubbing gets fixed NOW — slide screws loosened, shimmed, re-driven — because after the face frames go on, access gets worse and motivation gets weaker.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Fasten the plywood top with 1-1/4" brads and glue (screws hold better but show — your call on a piece this visible). Ease the front edge well; the backs of knees meet it every shoe-tying.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Measure the assembled front before you cut anything to final length: the frame gets a 72" top rail, a 72" bottom rail and four 13" stiles, which sums to 18" tall and matches the bench exactly, but the stile POSITIONS have to land over the frame uprights, not on the listed spacing — the plan's face frame implies wider openings than the carcass actually has. Pocket-hole the whole frame together flat on a bench with a 3/4" setting and 1 1/4" screws, checking the diagonals; building it as one unit and then hanging it is why the reveals come out even, and it is much harder to fix piece by piece on the front of the bench. Then glue the completed frame to the carcass and tack it with brads, clamping across the face while the glue sets.

steps.title: Face the drawers
steps.body: Size each drawer face frame to the opening it covers rather than to the listed lengths, which the cut list itself marks approximate — measure each of the three openings separately, since they will not be identical. Build the three 1x3 frames flat and check them for square, then hang each one on its box holding an even 1/8" gap all the way around; stacks of playing cards make good shims, and setting the bottom shims first lets the face sit on them while you fix it. Tack with brads and glue, then pull the drawer and add a screw from inside the box into the back of the face so it cannot work loose. Add the knobs, sand and finish the whole piece, easing the front edge of the top well — the backs of knees meet it every time someone ties a shoe.

images.alt: Six-foot mudroom bench with three drawers on full-extension slides
